title: “Mono” Went Away; “Metro” Arrived!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has launched the trial run of Chennai's Metro Rail. Every newspaper carried the story prominently on its front page. It is a Rs 14,600 crore project. Yet the Chief Minister who launched it did not utter a single word.

Unlike the many projects she launches daily through video conference from the Secretariat, she came in person for this one. Had she spoken at the launch, would she not have had to say under which government and in which year the project began? And so, without any ceremonial address, she waved the flag and launched the Metro Rail trial run.

Though she did not speak there, the Tamil Nadu government issued a note about the project. It described its details but carefully concealed the government under which it began. This project was begun by the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam government when I was Chief Minister.

Immediately after the DMK assumed office under my leadership in 2006, the Tamil Nadu Cabinet decided to implement the Chennai Metro Rail project. It was conceived as a fast, reliable, comfortable, efficient, modern and economical public-transport system, and as a long-term answer to growing transport demand. The DMK government ordered a detailed project report, and that work was entrusted to the Delhi Metro Rail Corporation.

The scheme was declared a “special initiative” under my direct supervision. It was later transferred to the charge of Thambi Stalin when he became Deputy Chief Minister. The detailed project report was received on 1 November 2007 and approved by the Tamil Nadu Cabinet on 7 November 2007. To implement it, the Tamil Nadu government created a special public-sector company, Chennai Metro Rail Limited, and registered it under the Indian Companies Act on 3 December 2007.

The project estimate was Rs 14,600 crore. Financial assistance from the Japanese government's international cooperation agency would cover 59 per cent of the cost. The loan agreement between the Union government and the Japanese government was signed in Tokyo on 21 November 2008. The Union government would provide 15 per cent of the cost as equity and five per cent as a loan. The state government would provide 15 per cent as equity and 5.78 per cent as a subordinate loan.

At its meeting on 28 January 2009, the Union Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs approved the Chennai Metro Rail project. It is being built along two corridors covering 45.1 kilometres. The first runs 23.1 kilometres from Washermanpet to Chennai Airport, and the second 22 kilometres from Central to St Thomas Mount. Each corridor is being built to modern standards with two tracks, one in each direction.
The Metro Rail project was thus pursued energetically and launched under the DMK government. But what did Jayalalithaa say about it after taking office?

In the Governor's Address read on 3 June 2011, immediately after the AIADMK assumed office, the government announced: “The government will implement a Monorail project integrated with the existing transport systems of Chennai. In the first phase it will cover 111 kilometres and will gradually be expanded to 300 kilometres. Appropriate studies will also be undertaken to implement Monorail projects in cities such as Coimbatore, Madurai and Tiruchirappalli to reduce traffic congestion.”

Then, in the Budget read on 4 August 2011, Finance Minister O. Panneerselvam said the necessary funding source for launching the Monorail quickly would be identified within that year. Was it identified?

Again, the Governor's Address of 30 January 2012 said, “I am pleased that the tendering work for implementing the Monorail project is progressing rapidly.” In the Budget read on 26 March 2012, they gave elaborate details of four Monorail routes. The minister concerned later repeated the same thing as though it were a new announcement.

They have kept saying tenders will be invited. But it is also said that tenders were once invited and could not be awarded because the “conditions” were not settled and problems arose in between. Then *Dinamani* reported on 16 June 2012 that there would be thirty-four Monorail stations and that a consultation chaired by the minister had lasted more than two hours.

In short, Monorail was a rival scheme announced out of Jayalalithaa's habitual hostility, because she did not want to accept the Metro Rail project introduced by the DMK government. Even after two and a half years, it remains at the stage of examining tenders. Experts, including senior official E. Sreedharan, issued reports explaining with evidence the differences between the two projects and why Metro Rail was superior to Monorail. Jayalalithaa would not listen then.
The AIADMK government halted the expansion of Chennai Metro Rail midway. In an interview in Chennai on 11 April 2012, E. Sreedharan, Principal Adviser to the Delhi Metro Rail Corporation, said:

> By abandoning the second phase of Chennai Metro Rail, the Tamil Nadu government is making a grave mistake. Three months ago I wrote to the Chief Secretary about this, but the government has not replied. Its decision to abandon the expansion is distressing. Chennai Metro Rail Limited is carrying out the work professionally and very well. Because it understands the value of expansion, it wants the project expanded.

> Studies must begin now if the Metro Rail is to be extended. The study itself will take eighteen months. Approvals from the Union government, the Ministry of Urban Development and the Tamil Nadu government will take another eighteen months. That makes more than three years. Such a large gap between the first and second phases is not good.

> Delhi Metro covers 65 kilometres in its first phase and 124.63 kilometres in its second, making it a sound commercial model upon which Metro projects in five major cities are being designed. But under the present plan, Chennai Metro covers only 45 kilometres. It will not be commercially viable unless many important areas are connected to the system. The state government's silence about extension to Tiruvottiyur is also worrying.

> Delay beyond the planned schedule will itself undermine the project. With a Metro project, time is money. The loss of a single day means the loss of several crores of rupees. Chennai is now a very large city, and no system other than Metro Rail can function as efficiently and economically. Monorail can carry only one-fourth as many passengers as Metro Rail. It also costs fifty per cent more to operate. It is an extremely expensive feeder service.

> Metro Rail, as a mass-transit system, must be treated as the backbone of urban transport. The Monorail being built in Mumbai costs Rs 110 crore per kilometre, while Metro Rail can carry three times as many passengers. Urban systems such as Metro Rail are capital-intensive, and state governments must invest in them with long-term needs in mind.

> The Tamil Nadu government should immediately extend the Metro work into second and third phases. Monorail is a poor arrangement for developed metropolitan cities. It can carry only a quarter of Metro Rail's passengers. Monorail has been proven unsuccessful in most cities. Metro Rail is the right system for moving large numbers of people. Monorail is an ordinary system with limited capacity and is not a successful model. Even in Japan, where it is now used, it operates only in small cities. Major cities such as Chennai and Mumbai require Metro Rail.
> Metro Rail is also less expensive. Compared with Metro Rail, a Monorail project costs fifty per cent more.
The AIADMK government paid no heed to this opinion, offered by Thiru E. Sreedharan on the basis of his experience.

Just as it stalled the Sethusamudram project and the Maduravoyal–Port elevated expressway, it stalled the expansion of Metro Rail. The same people have now launched its trial run and are trying to claim pride as though they themselves created the project.
But the people of Tamil Nadu know very well the truth: under whose government the Metro Rail project began, and who rejected that Metro project and spoke against it.
With affection,
M. K.
8 November 2013
